author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
The Fourth Passage. In What Sense God Only is Good. With God to Be Good and to Be Himself are the Same Thing.
title: The Fourth Passage. In What Sense God Only is Good. With God to Be Good and to Be Himself are the Same Thing.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Chapter XIV.—(32.)

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: The Fourth Passage.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: In What Sense God Only is Good.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: With God to Be Good and to Be Himself are the Same Thing.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: “They likewise,” says he, “quote what the Saviour says: ‘Why callest thou me good?

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: There is none good save one, that is, God?’”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: This statement, however, he makes no attempt whatever to explain; all he does is to oppose to it sundry other passages which seem to contradict it, which he adduces to show that man, too, is good.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Here are his remarks: “We must answer this text with another, in which the same Lord says, ‘A good man out of the good treasure of his heart bringeth forth good things.’

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: And again: ‘He maketh His sun to rise on the good and on the evil.’

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Then in another passage it is written, ‘For the good things are created from the beginning;’ and yet again, ‘They that are good shall dwell in the land.’”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Now to all this we must say in answer, that the passages in question must be understood in the same sense as the former one, “There is none good, save one, that is, God.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Either because all created things, although God made them very good, are yet, when compared with their Creator, not good, being in fact incapable of any comparison with Him.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: For in a transcendent, and yet very proper sense, He said of Himself, “I Am that I Am.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: The statement therefore before us, “None is good save one, that is, God,” is used in some such way as that which is said of John, “He was not that light;” although the Lord calls him “a lamp,” just as He says to His disciples: “Ye are the light of the world: . . .neither do men light a lamp and put it under a bushel.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Still, in comparison with that light which is “the true light which lighteth every man that cometh into the world,” he was not light.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Or else, because the very sons of God even, when compared with themselves as they shall hereafter become in their eternal perfection, are good in such a way that they still remain also evil.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Although I should not have dared to say this of them (for who would be so bold as to call them evil who have God for their Father?) unless the Lord had Himself said: “If ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more shall your Father which is in heaven give good things to them that ask Him?”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Of course, by applying to them the words, “your Father,” He proved that they were already sons of God; and yet at the same time He did not hesitate to say that they were “evil.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Your author, however, does not explain to us how they are good, whilst yet “there is none good save one, that is, God.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Accordingly the man who asked “what good thing he was to do,” was admonished to seek Him by whose grace he might be good; to whom also to be good is nothing else than to be Himself, because He is unchangeably good, and cannot be evil at all.
